paraphrase: Northern lords secretly resent the Boltons and Freys over the Red Wedding; Wyman Manderly is confirmed to be plotting against the Boltons, and the Umbers and Dustins are suspected of the same.
Jeyne Poole is being passed off by the Boltons as Arya Stark and married to Ramsay; Mance Rayder and six wildling women infiltrate Winterfell to rescue her.
A Ryswell soldier is found dead in Winterfell with a broken neck, half-buried in the snow -- the first of the murders.
A dead Frey squire is found naked in the castle graveyard -- the second murder.
A dead Flint soldier is found in the stables -- the third death.
Ramsay's friend Yellow Dick is found murdered and mutilated, his severed member forced into his own mouth so hard it broke his teeth -- the fourth murder.
'Little' Walder Frey, the nine-year-old grandson of Lord Walder Frey, is found murdered -- the fifth victim, which escalates tensions dramatically.
The Freys accuse Wyman Manderly of killing Little Walder, and the Freys and Manderlys begin fighting amongst themselves.

To stop his army from tearing itself apart, Roose Bolton orders the Freys and Manderlys to march out and attack Stannis -- which is actually what Stannis wants, since (per ASX's Pink Letter video) he appears to be setting a trap for the Freys.

The five victims aren't random: each is closely tied to a lord loyal to the Boltons -- a Ryswell groom, a Frey squire, Little Walder, and Ramsay's own friend -- since the Ryswells and Freys are the only houses that seem genuinely loyal to the Boltons.
The Flint soldier doesn't fit the targeted pattern, since the Flints are a minor, little-known house -- so he may simply have been 'kicked by a horse' in the stables rather than murdered.
verbatim: Then, before the day was done, a crossbowman sworn to the Flints turned up in the stables with a broken skull. Kicked by a horse, Lord Ramsay declared. A club, more like, Theon decided.

paraphrase: Winterfell's lords suspect the murderer works for Stannis or for Wyman Manderly; some suspect Theon himself, but rule him out because Ramsay's torture has left him too starved, frail, and psychologically broken to overpower and kill five men.
Amid the murders, Theon wanders Winterfell alone at night and encounters a mysterious man in a hooded cloak.
The Hooded Man recognizes Theon and calls him a 'Turncloak,' reflecting how many northmen despise 'Theon Turncloak' for betraying the Starks and seizing Winterfell.

The Hooded Man also calls Theon a 'Kinslayer' -- an unusual charge, since it rests on the belief that Theon murdered his 'foster brothers' Bran and Rickon, which counts as kinslaying in northmen's eyes even without a blood tie.
The Hooded Man puts a hand on his dagger as though he might attack Theon, but when Theon shows him his disfigured, tortured hands, the man only laughs and leaves Theon to his torment.
Theon suspects the Hooded Man might be the Winterfell murderer, since he's prowling the castle grounds at night carrying a cloak and a dagger.
Only three characters in the whole series call Theon a 'kinslayer': the Hooded Man, the wildling Rowan, and Crowfood Umber, a northman working for Stannis.
Crowfood Umber conveniently happens to be waiting just outside Winterfell to grab Theon and Jeyne at the exact moment Rowan helps them escape, suggesting Rowan and Crowfood are connected.
Rowan clearly hates Theon for supposedly killing Bran and Rickon -- she spits in his face and says he wasn't punished enough.
It's strange for a wildling -- traditional enemies of the Starks -- that Rowan makes a respectful comment about 'Lord Eddard's words.'
verbatim: Rowan gave him a hard look. “You have no right to mouth Lord Eddard’s words. Not you. Not ever. After what you did—”

spoken_quote: was carried off by wildlings thirty years ago
paraphrase: The books mention that Crowfood Umber had a daughter who 'was carried off by wildlings thirty years ago,' raising the possibility that Rowan is that daughter.
verbatim: The Night’s Watch takes no part, Jon thought, but another voice within him said, Words are not swords. “The elder of the Greatjon’s uncles. Crowfood, they call him. A crow once took him for dead and pecked out his eye. He caught the bird in his fist and bit its head off. When Mors was young he was a fearsome fighter. His sons died on the Trident, his wife in childbed. His only daughter was carried off by wildlings thirty years ago.”

paraphrase: If Rowan is Crowfood's daughter, raised among the Umbers (Stark loyalists), that would explain both her hatred of Theon and her respect for Ned Stark, and why she'd risk her life to save the girl she believes is Ned's daughter.
The Hooded Man appears on the very same night Crowfood arrives outside Winterfell's walls, suggesting they may have arrived together.
Crowfood calls Theon 'turncloak' and 'kinslayer,' just as the Hooded Man does -- a verbal parallel linking the two.
Rowan and the wildling infiltrators were sent to Winterfell by Melisandre, who serves Stannis, and Crowfood also serves Stannis -- tying the whole possible conspiracy back to Stannis's camp.

Candidate: Robett Glover, brother of Lord Galbart Glover. The Glover brothers fought for Robb Stark; Roose Bolton later betrayed Robett by sending him into a trap at Duskendale, and Robett's wife and children are held hostage on the Iron Islands by Asha Greyjoy.
In Book 5, Robett Glover is already working with Wyman Manderly in his secret anti-Bolton plan to help Stannis, so he could plausibly have come to Winterfell with Wyman and become the Hooded Man.
When Robett plots with Wyman Manderly, he wears a cloak and uses a dagger -- matching the Hooded Man's cloak and dagger.
It would make sense for Robett to hate 'Theon Turncloak,' since the Greyjoy invasion of the north cost him his family and home.
Counter-evidence: Robett Glover knows that Bran and Rickon are actually alive, so it doesn't make sense for him to call Theon 'Kinslayer' for supposedly killing them.

Weak rebuttal attempts: Theon could be called a kinslayer for getting ironborn men killed at Moat Cailin, or via the separate theory that Theon actually killed his own sons -- but neither fully justifies Robett using that specific word.
Candidate: Harwin, one of Ned Stark's household guards, who helped teach Arya, Jon, and Robb to ride -- a trusted, loyal Stark servant.

In Book 1, Harwin was sent on a mission with Beric Dondarrion and Thoros of Myr, was attacked, and the group became the Brotherhood Without Banners; Harwin later has an emotional reunion with Arya when she joins the Brotherhood.

By Book 4, Harwin is with the Brotherhood group led by Lady Stoneheart -- the resurrected Catelyn Stark -- who is searching for her daughter Arya and questioning captives about her, and would by now have heard that 'Arya' has married Ramsay Bolton.

Harwin is a plausible choice for Stoneheart to send to rescue 'Arya,' since he knows Arya, knows Winterfell, and is loyal to the Starks; on realizing Ramsay's bride isn't really Arya, he could have become the Hooded Man, murdering Boltons and getting involved with Rowan and Wyman's conspiracy.
Since Harwin grew up alongside Theon and the Stark children, it makes sense he'd see Bran and Rickon as Theon's 'brothers' and hate Theon as a kinslayer.
Counter-evidence: Harwin and Theon knew each other for years, so why doesn't Theon recognize him? The Hooded Man's hood doesn't hide his face -- the two come 'face-to-face' during the encounter.

Rebuttal: Theon fails to recognize even his own sister Asha earlier in the story, and Arya herself has trouble recognizing Harwin because he's thinner and has grown a new beard -- so it's plausible Theon simply didn't recognize him.
The video notes Harwin has a deeper emotional connection to the Stark characters than Robett Glover does, which would make for a more meaningful reveal if Harwin turns out to be the Hooded Man.
Candidate: Hallis Mollen ('Hal'), another Winterfell guard, who guarded Catelyn Stark during Robb's war until she sent him north with Ned Stark's bones to be interred in the Winterfell crypt.

Hal is never confirmed to have reached Winterfell -- Catelyn suspected he might have been blocked at Moat Cailin, and in Winterfell, Barbrey Dustin says she's watching for Ned's bones (intending to feed them to her dogs) but hasn't found them either, leaving Hal's fate and location open.

If blocked at Moat Cailin, Hal could plausibly have ended up at nearby Greywater Watch, home of Howland Reed -- the one man alive who knows Jon Snow is the son of Prince Rhaegar -- and Robett Glover's brother Galbart was also sent there and knows about Robb Stark's will naming Jon King in the North.
If Hal eventually made it to Winterfell, he could be playing the role of the Hooded Man -- killing Bolton soldiers and trying to return Ned's bones.
Like Harwin, Hal grew up around Theon and the Stark children, so it would make sense for him to hate Theon as a 'Kinslayer' too.
Counter-evidence: Hal's personality is a poor fit for a secret hooded mission -- he's known for having 'a loose tongue' and for loudly stating the obvious.
Some fans think Brynden 'Blackfish' Tully or Benjen Stark could be the Hooded Man, though the video doesn't elaborate on either candidate.

Alternative view: it could really be almost anyone who loved the Starks, and maybe the point is that it doesn't matter who's specifically under the hood -- he could just be an unnamed, unknown northman.

This ties to a broader theme that even though the Starks are defeated, their legacy lives on -- 'the north remembers' Ned Stark's leadership, which is why so many northmen still fight and die in his name and are trying to save his daughter; the Hooded Man may embody that loyalty.
The video argues that whoever the Hooded Man is, he's probably NOT the actual Winterfell murderer -- the wildlings are responsible instead.

Jon Snow and Melisandre are the ones who sent Mance Rayder and the wildling women to rescue Jeyne, believing her to be Arya -- but Jon reasons the wildlings wouldn't risk their lives just to save 'Arya Stark' alone, so they must have another hidden purpose, likely helping Stannis by killing soldiers.
The wildling women are shown to be capable killers -- they kill several guards during Theon and Jeyne's escape from Winterfell.
When Theon accuses the wildling women of the murders, Rowan denies killing Little Walder but effectively admits that they killed Yellow Dick and the others.
During the escape, the wildling women use their sexuality to lure guards into a vulnerable position before killing them -- likely the same method used on the earlier murder victims.

This fits the murder details: Yellow Dick's severed member was found in his own mouth, and the Frey squire was found 'naked' -- both consistent with the women luring the men with sex before killing them.

Little Walder's murder may have a different culprit: his young cousin Big Walder Frey, who is only nine but ambitious and brutal, constantly talks about House Frey's line of succession, says he's determined to become Lord of the Crossing, and talks casually about murdering other Freys to get there.
When Little Walder's body is found, Big Walder's gloves are described as 'caked with his cousin's blood' -- a classic guilt symbol pointing to him as the killer.
verbatim: “Under that ruined keep, my lord,” replied Big Walder. “The one with the old gargoyles.” The boy’s gloves were caked with his cousin’s blood. “I told him not to go out alone, but he said he had to find a man who owed him silver.”

paraphrase: Overall conclusion: the wildlings killed Yellow Dick, the Ryswell groom, and the Frey squire as part of their conspiracy to aid Stannis, Big Walder killed Little Walder for succession, and the Flint soldier was probably just an accident -- 'murder mystery solved.'
If the wildlings and Big Walder are the real murderers, it raises the question of what purpose the Hooded Man actually serves in the story, suggesting he may carry a deeper, more symbolic meaning.
Theon's arc in this book is about escaping his broken 'Reek' identity and reclaiming his true self as 'Theon Greyjoy' -- Reek is weak, afraid, and obedient, while Theon was proud and strong.
The text states that 'Theon Greyjoy died,' and that Reek is a new man, born in Ramsay's dungeon.
verbatim: His head snapped up. “Who said that?” All he could see were the trees and the fog that covered them. The voice had been as faint as rustling leaves, as cold as hate. A god’s voice, or a ghost’s. How many died the day that he took Winterfell? How many more the day he lost it? The day that Theon Greyjoy died, to be reborn as Reek. Reek, Reek, it rhymes with shriek.

spoken_quote: full of ghosts for Theon Greyjoy
paraphrase: Theon is repeatedly haunted by 'ghosts' in Winterfell, which is described as 'full of ghosts for Theon Greyjoy' -- the people he betrayed or got killed there.
verbatim: Winterfell was full of ghosts for Theon Greyjoy.

paraphrase: Theon hears ghostly voices in the godswood and sees Bran's face appear in a weirwood tree.
When Theon meets the Hooded Man, he comes 'face-to-face' with him 'alone' in the snow, and -- strangely, given the circumstances -- Theon is 'not afraid.'
Theory: the Hooded Man could be a vision or hallucination in Theon's tortured mind -- a representation of his identity crisis, essentially the ghost of who Theon used to be.

The Hooded Man acts like the old, pre-torture Theon: confident, purposeful, and mocking -- he laughs at Theon's disfigured hand, much like the smug, cruel young Theon used to laugh at others with his sly smile and contemptuous laugh.
In Book 2, at the height of his pride and confidence, Theon himself wore a hooded cloak -- just like the Hooded Man.
The video compares the Hooded Man to ghosts in Shakespeare's Hamlet and Macbeth, who represent characters' inner conflicts, guilt, and doubts.

spoken_quote: The gods are not done with me
paraphrase: The Hooded Man could also represent Death itself: in the Faith of the Seven, the god of death appears as a hooded figure called the Stranger, and the Hooded Man threatens Theon much like death would -- but Theon defies him, saying 'The gods are not done with me,' choosing life and escaping Winterfell instead.
verbatim: “The gods are not done with me,” Theon answered, wondering if this could be the killer, the night walker who had stuffed Yellow Dick’s cock into his mouth and pushed Roger Ryswell’s groom off the battlements. Oddly, he was not afraid. He pulled the glove from his left hand. “Lord Ramsay is not done with me.”

paraphrase: Counter-evidence: the Hooded Man calls Theon a 'Kinslayer,' but Theon himself believes he isn't one, since he didn't actually kill Bran and Rickon -- so if the Hooded Man is purely a projection of Theon's own mind, it's odd that he'd accuse Theon of a crime Theon doesn't believe he committed.

Counter-theory: maybe Theon really is a kinslayer after all -- in Book 2, when Theon fails to find Bran and Rickon, he kills two other boys of the same age and burns their bodies, passing them off as Bran and Rickon's; their mother is a woman called the miller's wife, whom Theon mentions having slept with a couple of times.
This raises the speculative possibility that one or both of the boys Theon killed were actually his own sons.

spoken_quote: the gods hate kinslayers, even when they kill unknowing
paraphrase: In Book 2, Ygritte tells a story about a man who unknowingly killed his own father, saying 'the gods hate kinslayers, even when they kill unknowing' -- which the video connects thematically to Theon's near-incestuous attraction to his own sister Asha, calling it an 'Oedipal, Freudian' parallel.
verbatim: “Aye,” she said, “but the gods hate kinslayers, even when they kill unknowing. When Lord Stark returned from the battle and his mother saw Bael’s head upon his spear, she threw herself from a tower in her grief. Her son did not long outlive her. One o’ his lords peeled the skin off him and wore him for a cloak.”

spoken_quote: There were things too hurtful to remember
paraphrase: At one point Theon tries not to remember something about the boys he killed, thinking 'There were things too hurtful to remember' -- suggesting he may be suppressing the memory that they were his own sons.
verbatim: Ned Stark’s sons are all dead, Reek thought. Robb was murdered at the Twins, and Bran and Rickon … we dipped the heads in tar … His own head was pounding. He did not want to think about anything that had happened before he knew his name. There were things too hurtful to remember, thoughts almost as painful as Ramsay’s flaying knife …

paraphrase: Counter-evidence: there isn't much actual textual evidence that the miller's boys were Theon's sons, and if it were true, it likely would have been revealed by the point Reek becomes Theon again.

Broader thematic reading: the recurring 'kinslayer' question is really about exploring who Theon's true kin and family are -- the Starks or the ironborn -- and whether he betrayed the only people who ever loved him.

Final counter to the ghost/hallucination theory: if Theon didn't actually slay any kin, it doesn't make sense for a ghost/projection of his own mind to falsely call him 'Kinslayer' -- reopening the possibility that the Hooded Man is a real, external person after all.
